message:
feat(fnp-python): native recfunctions_rename_fields for flat structured dtypes (br-franken_numpy-hswh)

Replace the numpy.lib.recfunctions.rename_fields passthrough with a
Rust-driven rebuild of the structured dtype descriptor:

1. Iterate `source.dtype.names`.
2. For each field, fetch the (subdtype, offset) pair via
   `dtype[name]`. Nested structured fields (those whose sub-dtype
   also has .names) fall back to numpy so recfunctions' recursion
   handles them.
3. Look up the new name in the provided dict; unmapped names stay
   unchanged.
4. Assemble a new `[(new_name, field_dtype), ...]` descriptor and
   `np.dtype()` it.
5. Return `source.view(new_dtype)` — a zero-copy reinterpretation
   whose buffer aliases the source exactly.

This closes the pure-passthrough gap for rename_fields without
importing numpy.lib.recfunctions. numpy.dtype and .view() are still
used for the construction primitive, but the rename/descriptor logic
is owned by fnp-python.

Parity test recfunctions_family_matches_numpy_across_drop_rename_append_merge_and_u2s
still passes (the rename branch exercises a 3-field dict remap). Full
suite: 395/395 lib tests green on rch.

The other recfunctions (drop/append/merge/unstructured_to_structured)
remain passthrough — each has numpy-specific semantics (usemask paths,
fill-value handling, nested recursion) that aren't a one-function port.
